title: How Psychedelic Therapy Addresses Dissociation and Emotional Numbing
body:
How Psychedelic Therapy Addresses Dissociation and Emotional Numbing

1. Opening Hook - A Personal Encounter with Emotional Numbing

There was a time not so long ago when I felt like I was living behind a thick glass wall. I could see the world moving around me - friends laughing, conversations flowing, life happening - but I was locked in a still, numb bubble. My emotions were muted; joy felt distant, sadness even more so. It wasn’t that I was depressed in the usual way. It was more like my feelings had been packed away in a storage unit somewhere, inaccessible and silent. The word “dissociation” was tossed around in therapy sessions, but it felt clinical, too abstract to catch the weight of what I was experiencing.

Dissociation and emotional numbing are not just fancy psychological terms. They are lived realities for many of us, especially those who have weathered trauma, chronic stress, or deep loneliness. I remember nights where the numbness felt like a protective shield - the only way to survive unbearable pain or confusion. But that shield became a prison. I was disconnected from the part of myself that allowed vulnerability and connection. This disconnection is a tricky beast. It’s not always obvious to others, and sometimes it fools us into thinking we’re “fine” because we’re not falling apart in dramatic ways.

What brought me out of that fog was psychedelic therapy, a modality I initially approached with skepticism. But as I began to explore it, I found something I hadn’t in years: a way to feel again, deeply and honestly. The numbness began to thaw. The glass wall cracked and, eventually, shattered. I want to share that journey and unpack how psychedelics can gently, yet powerfully, address dissociation and emotional numbing. This isn’t about a quick fix or magic bullet. It’s about reconnecting to ourselves in a way that’s deep and lasting.

Before I came to this understanding, I read a lot, talked to experts, and experimented with integration practices. Books like The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk helped me understand how trauma physically lodges inside us, often manifesting as dissociation and emotional shutdown. And Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind gave me a roadmap of the science and history of psychedelics, revealing why these substances might be uniquely suited to heal these conditions.

This article is my invitation to anyone feeling stuck in this numb space to consider how psychedelic therapy might offer a doorway out. I will walk through the science, the research, practical advice, and integration strategies that helped me and many others come back to life emotionally.

2. Understanding Dissociation and Emotional Numbing: The Science Behind the Experience

Dissociation and emotional numbing often go hand in hand, but they are distinct experiences tied to how our brain processes trauma, stress, and overwhelming emotions. Dissociation is essentially a disconnection from reality or parts of our experience - an involuntary defense mechanism where the mind detaches from painful feelings or memories. Emotional numbing, on the other hand, is a dulling or absence of emotional response, where the person doesn’t feel joy, sadness, or maybe any intense emotion at all.

From a neuroscience perspective, these symptoms are linked to changes in brain networks responsible for self-awareness, emotional regulation, and trauma processing. The amygdala, a small almond-shaped structure deep in the brain, plays a big role in emotional reactivity. In dissociative states, the amygdala can become either hyperactive or, paradoxically, suppressed to keep overwhelming emotion at bay. Meanwhile, areas like the prefrontal cortex, which help regulate emotion and cognitive control, can become disrupted.

Rick Strassman’s and Carl Hart’s work on altered states of consciousness showed how the brain’s normal filters between perception and emotion can loosen, which is why dissociation feels like an “out-of-body” state. More recently, Robin Carhart-Harris and his team at Imperial College London have mapped how psychedelics like psilocybin "reset" the brain’s default mode network (DMN). The DMN is often overactive in depression and trauma-related conditions, locking people into rigid negative thinking and emotional avoidance.

When psychedelics temporarily quiet the DMN, it allows for new connections to form in the brain - connections that can help integrate traumatic memories and lost emotions. It’s a neurological chance to break out of the repetitive loops of dissociation and numbness. This mechanism aligns with what Stanislav Grof observed decades ago: that psychedelics provide a safe “emotional catharsis” by bypassing the usual defenses, letting buried feelings surface in a controlled way.

Studies also show that psychedelics raise levels of neuroplasticity - the brain’s ability to form and reorganize synaptic connections. This is critical because dissociation and emotional numbing often come from trauma-induced “freezing” of emotional circuits. Psychedelics help unfreeze these circuits, allowing new emotional experiences to take root. Understanding this brain science helps clarify why psychedelic therapy is more than just a hallucinogenic trip - it’s a deeply biological reset for emotional disconnection.

3. What the Research Says: Psychedelic Therapy and Healing Dissociation

The modern psychedelic renaissance has brought a surge of clinical research shedding light on how these substances affect trauma-related symptoms. One of the most cited studies for trauma and dissociation is Roland Griffiths’ 2016 trial where he administered psilocybin to patients struggling with depression and anxiety. While the primary focus wasn’t dissociation per se, many participants reported reduced emotional numbing and improved emotional sensitivity following treatment.

More direct research comes from work on PTSD patients. A 2021 study published in the Journal of Psychopharmacology by Michael Mithoefer and colleagues found that MDMA-assisted therapy significantly reduced PTSD symptoms, many of which include dissociation and emotional numbing. Although MDMA is not a classic psychedelic like psilocybin, it shares the quality of increasing emotional openness and connection.

When it comes to psilocybin, a 2020 pilot study by Carhart-Harris and his team showed that a single dose led to significant reductions in symptoms of treatment-resistant depression, which often coexists with dissociation and emotional detachment. Participants described a “reawakening” of feelings they hadn’t connected with in years. These subjective reports line up with neurological scans revealing decreased DMN activity and increased connectivity in emotional centers of the brain.

Stanislav Grof’s early research with LSD also highlighted the psychedelic’s ability to access dissociated memories and emotions safely. Grof’s concept of “holotropic breathwork” and psychedelic therapy parallels modern practices aimed at revisiting trauma in a container of safety and support. This approach helps patients move through dissociation by consciously experiencing the emotions that were previously buried.

Complementing these findings is Paul Stamets’ work on microdosing psilocybin, showing that even low doses can subtly improve emotional responsiveness and reduce feelings of numbness over time, although the research is still preliminary. The combined evidence points toward psychedelics as tools to re-engage our emotional lives, not by forcing feeling, but by creating the biological and psychological conditions to rediscover it naturally.

4. How to Approach Psychedelic Therapy for Dissociation and Emotional Numbing

If you’re reading this and wondering how to start engaging with psychedelic therapy for dissociation or emotional numbing, know that intent and preparation are key. These experiences emerge from deep wounds, so a safe container matters more than the dose or setting alone. The first step is connecting with a trained psychedelic therapist or guide who has experience with trauma and dissociation.

Therapists who offer this work will often employ gradual exposure. Instead of diving headfirst into a full-dose journey, they might start with microdosing or low doses to gently awaken emotional circuits without feeling overwhelmed. Preparing mentally through journaling, such as in a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound, helps clarify intentions and notice subtle emotional shifts.

One practical technique I suggest is approaching sessions with curiosity rather than expectation. Emotional numbing isn’t about “fixing” yourself instantly or forcing tears. It’s about opening a door enough to feel the air and light behind it. As Michael Pollan writes in How to Change Your Mind, psychedelics provide a “clean slate” for emotional experience, but it requires willingness to sit with discomfort and uncertainty.

Integration afterward is equally crucial. This means talking through the experience with a therapist, practicing mindfulness, and cultivating emotional awareness in day-to-day life. Simple grounding tools like breathwork, body scans, or somatic exercises can help reconnect with feelings safely. Trauma experts like Bessel van der Kolk remind us in The Body Keeps the Score that trauma isn’t just in the mind - it’s in the body, so physical practices matter.

Finally, patience is necessary. Emotional reconnection unfolds over weeks and months, not hours. Psychedelic therapy offers a powerful nudge, but the work is ongoing and often requires a supportive community or therapist to move through the ups and downs.

5. Common Challenges When Facing Dissociation in Psychedelic Therapy

Working with dissociation and emotional numbing in psychedelic therapy is not without its difficulties. One common challenge is that dissociation itself can be a barrier to fully engaging with the psychedelic experience. When the mind habitually disconnects from feelings or memories, psychedelic states can sometimes trigger intense avoidance or even panic.

I’ve witnessed sessions where clients, confronted suddenly with raw emotion or fragmented memories, instinctively pull back into numbness or dissociative detachment. This can feel frightening, like losing control. The fear of “losing oneself” is real and must be acknowledged openly. In these moments, the presence of a compassionate, experienced guide is everything. They provide the anchor to remind the person that these feelings are temporary and manageable.

Another issue is the expectation that psychedelics will bring instant emotional healing. Some people expect an immediate flood of feeling or catharsis. When that doesn’t happen, frustration and discouragement can set in. It’s vital to remember that emotional numbing often developed decades ago as a survival tool. Psychedelics gently loosen these defenses but don’t yank them away forcefully.

Physical discomfort can also arise. Because trauma and dissociation live in the body, psychedelic sessions sometimes bring up somatic sensations - tightness, shaking, nausea - that can feel overwhelming. Approaching these sensations as messages from the body rather than threats helps. Practices like grounding to the breath or gentle movement can ease the intensity.

Finally, there’s the challenge of integration. After the heightened emotional state of a session, many people struggle to carry the insights and feelings into daily routines. Without support, the progress can feel like it evaporates. This is why establishing a post-session routine that includes journaling (a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound is handy here), therapy, or community support makes a huge difference.

In my own experience and that of others, patience, self-compassion, and setting realistic goals help work through these challenges. Dissociation isn’t a flaw; it’s a sign of survival. Psychedelic therapy invites us to meet it with respect and curiosity.

6. Integration and Living Beyond the Session: Sustaining Emotional Reconnection

The psychedelic session often feels like a spark, but the real work happens afterward - when you return to everyday life with a new emotional toolkit. Integration is the process of folding the experience into your daily reality, making it sustainable and meaningful.

In the context of dissociation and emotional numbing, integration means cultivating regular practices that nurture emotional connection. Mindfulness meditation is one such practice, teaching us to be present with feelings rather than avoiding them. Over time, mindfulness helps reduce the reflexive dissociative response to emotional discomfort.

Body-based therapies are also powerful. Bessel van der Kolk’s The Body Keeps the Score makes clear that trauma is stored in the body’s tissues. Practices like yoga, somatic experiencing, or simple daily stretches can slowly release stored tension and open access to feeling.

Journaling is another essential tool. Writing down thoughts and feelings after sessions helps clarify insights that might otherwise dissipate. Having a dedicated space, like a special Journaling Notebook Leather Bound, can encourage consistency and deepen self-reflection.

Connection with others is equally important. Dissociation can isolate us emotionally, so finding safe spaces to share your experience - whether with trusted friends, support groups, or therapists - helps rebuild trust and openness. These relationships provide feedback, encouragement, and the reminder that feeling vulnerable is human.

Finally, the long-term perspective matters. Emotional reconnection is not a straight line. There will be ups and downs, moments of breakthrough and moments of retreat. Embracing this ebb and flow, without judgment, is part of healing. Psychedelic therapy can light the path, but the journey belongs to you.

7. Closing Reflection: The Gift Hidden in Dissociation

When I reflect on my own experience with dissociation and emotional numbing, I realize there’s a strange paradox: these states are both a wound and a witness, a shelter and a warning. The mind creates dissociation to protect us from unbearable pain, yet in doing so, it cuts us off from the rich spectrum of human feeling. Psychedelic therapy doesn’t just breach this wall; it invites us to gently examine why it was built in the first place.

There’s power in sitting with dissociation as a teacher, not just a symptom. When psychedelics quiet the mental noise and open new pathways, they allow us to peer into the hidden chambers of our psyche. Sometimes what we find isn’t pretty - painful memories, fears, or parts of ourselves we avoided - but acknowledging these parts is the first step to reclaiming wholeness.

Stanislav Grof’s insights connect here: the psychedelic journey is like entering the deep layers of our personal unconscious. It’s a return to the self, but a self that is wiser for having faced its shadows. When emotional numbing lifts, what emerges is often a fragile, tender connection to life - a connection that must be handled with care and cultivated patiently.

In that sense, dissociation is not an enemy but a call for healing. Psychedelic therapy offers a way to answer that call, not by rushing toward feeling but by creating a space where feeling can emerge on its own terms. This space invites us into the messy, beautiful complexity of being human.

I encourage anyone reading this who feels stuck in numbness to hold onto that idea: your emotional self is waiting quietly beneath the surface. With the right support and tools, it can be found again. And when it is, the world suddenly shines in color once more.
